summary: Furio Dondero is a colorful Nevada-born individual who is now elderly but still alive. We've always been associated in mining together, holding a quarter interest in five mining claims in the Ruby Hills of Eureka County. His involvement in these claims has been part of a partnership with other distinguished Nevadans.

Retired General Dale Smith is one of my partners in mining claims in Nevada. His father was 'Long Tom' Smith, a wonderful individual I knew when he was state engineer of Nevada. Dale Smith himself has been a key figure in our group holding patented mining claims in Eureka County.

Hugh Shamberger was also a state engineer of Nevada for many years and is one of the four partners holding mining claims with me in the Ruby Hills. We've held these patented claims together, which have provided some tax benefits despite not making money from mining. Hugh's role as state engineer and partner has been significant in our mining endeavors.

The Ruby Hills in Eureka County is where our mining claims are located. We held thirteen claims there, called the 'Lucky Thirteen,' and sold eight to a mining company that tried to develop them but failed due to water pumping costs. The area is rich in lead, zinc, and some silver, and I hope it will have a mining future again.

Eureka County is the location of the Ruby Hills mining district where I hold mining claims with partners. The county has a fine mining past, and although our claims haven't been profitable, the area's mineral wealth suggests it might see mining activity again. Our patented claims there have been a small but legitimate income tax deduction for us.

Lovelock was where my father ran a grocery store located in what is now the Turrillas building. We lived there while I attended the lower grades of school before moving to the Fairview district. Lovelock was also near my mother's family ranch and school system, making it an important place in my early life.

After my father bought a ranch in the upper Lovelock Valley, I attended school in the Fairview district for grades three to five. I rode a horse about a mile to a two-room, eight-grade school with two teachers. Our home was about six miles from Lovelock, and the Fairview area was where a devastating fire destroyed our home around 1918, prompting our move to Fallon.

We moved to Fallon, Nevada around 1918 or 1919 after a fire destroyed our home in Fairview. My father bought a grocery store called Peoples Brothers there, which was run by early farmers from the Fernley and Fallon districts. I entered fifth grade in Fallon, finished grammar school and high school there, and later attended the University of Nevada.

One evening around 1918, after attending a motion picture show in Lovelock, we saw the northern skies lit up beautifully and thought it was the aurora borealis. As we got closer to home in Fairview, we realized the light was actually from a fire that had burned our house to the ground. The aurora borealis was a memorable but tragic backdrop to that event in my childhood.

Peoples Brothers was a grocery store in Fallon, Nevada, run by early farmers from the Fernley and Fallon districts. After our home burned down, my father seized the opportunity to buy this store, which was the main reason we moved to Fallon around 1918 or 1919. This store marked a new chapter in our family's life and livelihood.

I attended the University of Nevada after finishing high school in Fallon. My mother also attended the University of Nevada for two years before becoming a schoolteacher. The university has been an important part of our family's educational history and my own development.

My mother, Isabel Welch, was born in Unionville, Nevada, and lived to be seventy-seven years old. She was the only girl among six or seven siblings and served as the family historian. She lost many family letters and valuables in the fire that destroyed our home, which was a great loss to our family history.

Unionville, Nevada, is where my mother, Isabel Welch, was born and where my aunt Ethel Welch was also born. It was part of my mother's early life before she moved to Lovelock. The town connects to our family's roots on my mother's side.

Brechin, Scotland, is where my maternal grandfather, Robert Welch, was born. My mother told me he was a professor at the University of Edinburgh, though I was unable to verify that during a visit to Edinburgh. Brechin is part of my mother's Scottish heritage.

My mother believed her father, Robert Welch, was a professor at the University of Edinburgh in Scotland. I visited Edinburgh years ago to try to confirm this but found no record of him as a professor. Despite this, the university remains a significant part of our family's lore and Scottish ancestry.

Robert Welch was my maternal grandfather, born in Brechin, Scotland. My mother said he was a professor at the University of Edinburgh, though I could not verify this. He later had a small ranch outside Lovelock, Nevada, where my mother grew up.

Winnemucca, Nevada, is where my aunt Ethel Welch lived her lifetime. She was married to my uncle Will Welch, my mother's brother. This town is part of my extended family's history on my mother's side.
